sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 140, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ACTION7 merges the trail with the background (fills 12 gap cells), effectively "extending" the trail downward to row 41. This might be a "confirm" or "fill" action. Level not completed, so current state isn't the goal.
- Plan: Try moving agent to different positions, then ACTION7. Or try LEFT/RIGHT to move the trail horizontally. First test LEFT from current position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 139 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT moved the agent left but the trail expanded from px=804 to px=816 (12 more cells). The trail is now at c=17-46, same as before. But the agent moved from col 11 to col 11... wait, the agent was already at col 11. Let me re-read.
Actually, looking at the output: "Agent: row=12, col=11" - the agent is still at col 11. But the c region expanded from px=804 to px=816. So LEFT added 12 cells to the c region.
Wait, but the c region is still at c=17-46. So the 12 new cells must be somewhere else. Let me check the before/after more carefully.
Actually, looking at the image, the trail (gray bar) appears shorter now. Before ACTION7, the trail was at rows 12-13, cols 17-46. After ACTION7, the trail merged with the background (rows 12-41, cols 17-46). After LEFT, the trail is now... hmm, the image shows the trail is shorter.
Let me check the segmentation more carefully. The c region is px=816 r=12-41 c=17-46. Before LEFT, it was px=804 r=12-41 c=17-46. So 12 cells were added, but the bounding box is the same. This means the c region now has more cells within the same bounding box.
Wait, but the image shows the trail is shorter. Let me look at the image again... Actually, the trail in the image appears to end earlier (around col 38-40 instead of col 46). So maybe the trail shortened?
Hmm, but the segmentation says c=17-46, which is the same as before. Let me check if there are multiple c regions or if the segmentation is different.
Actually, I think the issue is that the trail (the patterned part at rows 12-13) shortened, but the dark background (rows 16-41) expanded to fill the gap. So the total c region size increased (804 -> 816), but the visible trail at the top shortened.
Let me check the trail specifically. Before LEFT, the trail was part of the merged c region (rows 12-41). After LEFT, maybe the trail portion (rows 12-13) shortened, and the background portion (rows 16-41) expanded.
Actually, I need to look at this more carefully. Let me check the ascii representation of the trail region.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT changed the c region (804→816 cells) but agent stayed at col 11. Trail may have shortened while background expanded. Need to understand LEFT/RIGHT mechanics.
Plan: Check the trail region in ascii to see what changed, then try RIGHT to reverse it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check trail region in ascii before and after LEFT
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

# Print rows 12-16 of ascii to see trail
print("Before LEFT (rows 12-16):")
before_lines = before.ascii.split('\n')
for i in range(12, min(17, len(before_lines))):
    print(f"  row {i}: {before_lines[i][10:50]}")

print("\nAfter LEFT (rows 12-16):")
after_lines = after.ascii.split('\n')
for i in range(12, min(17, len(after_lines))):
    print(f"  row {i}: {after_lines[i][10:50]}")

# Also check the full c region segmentation
print("\n=== c nodes in after frame ===")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] > 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"c: px={n['pixels']} r={r_min}-{r_max} c={c_min}-{c_max}")

# Check agent position
print("\n=== Agent in after frame ===")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"M: px={n['pixels']} r={r_min}-{r_max} c={c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see what happened. Looking at rows 14-15:
Before LEFT:
row 14: ...wgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwcccccc...
row 15: ...gwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgcccccc...
After LEFT:
row 14: ...wgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwcccccccccccc...
row 15: ...gwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgcccccccccccc...
So LEFT removed some of the w/g pattern cells from the right side and replaced them with c cells. The pattern shortened by about 6-8 characters on the right.
The pattern is: wgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgww (before) -> wgwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgww (after)
Before: the pattern extends from col 17 to about col 46 (30 chars)
After: the pattern extends from col 17 to about col 38 (22 chars)
So LEFT shortens the pattern by removing cells from the right. The removed cells become c (charcoal), which is the same color as the trail/background.
This means LEFT/RIGHT control the length of the pattern (the w/g checkerboard strip).
Now, what's the goal? Maybe the goal is to set the pattern length to match something, like the number of blocks or the position of the blocks?
The blocks are at cols 42-45. The pattern currently ends at about col 38. If I use RIGHT, the pattern would extend further right, potentially reaching the blocks.
Let me think about what the "correct" pattern length might be. The blocks are at cols 42-45. If the pattern should reach the blocks, it should end at col 41 (just before the blocks start at col 42).
Currently, after LEFT, the pattern ends at about col 38. So I need to use RIGHT 3 times to extend it to col 41.
But wait, let me first understand the pattern better. The pattern is at rows 14-15, and it's a checkerboard of w and g cells. The pattern seems to be aligned with the G bars on the left (cols 13-14).
Actually, looking at the ascii, the pattern starts at col 17 (after the g cell at col 16). The G bars are at cols 13-14. The agent is at cols 11-16.
Let me think about this differently. The pattern length might need to match the vertical position of the agent or the G bars.
Actually, let me just try RIGHT to see if it extends the pattern, and then try to find the "correct" length by testing different configurations.
But first, let me note that the agent didn't move (still at col 11). So LEFT/RIGHT don't move the agent, they only change the pattern length.
Wait, but earlier when I moved LEFT, the agent was at col 11 and stayed at col 11. But the trail changed. So LEFT/RIGHT only affect the trail/pattern, not the agent position.
Let me try RIGHT to extend the pattern and see if the level completes at some specific pattern length.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT/RIGHT control the pattern length (w/g checkerboard at rows 14-15). LEFT shortens pattern from right, RIGHT likely extends it. Agent stays at col 11. Pattern currently ends ~col 38, blocks at cols 42-45.
Plan: Try RIGHT to extend pattern toward blocks. Test if pattern reaching blocks (col 41) completes the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to extend the pattern
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check pattern length after RIGHT
after = current_frame
lines = after.ascii.split('\n')
print("After RIGHT (rows 14-15):")
for i in [14, 15]:
    print(f"  row {i}: {lines[i][10:50]}")

# Count pattern cells (w and g at rows 14-15, cols 17+)
pattern_end = 0
for i in [14, 15]:
    row_str = lines[i]
    for j in range(17, 50):
        if row_str[j] in 'wg':
            pattern_end = max(pattern_end, j)
        else:
            break
print(f"Pattern ends at col: {pattern_end}")

# Check if level completed
if last_action_result.get('level_completed') or last_action_result.get('done'):
    print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
